mapping.a_element: spatially heterogeneous, patchy soil (pores of varying structure/chemistry)
mapping.b_element: spatially heterogeneous landscape composed of two habitat types

mapping.a_element: bacterial populations living and growing within the environment
mapping.b_element: the two competing species whose populations are tracked

mapping.a_element: coarse-graining from the single pore up through the mesoscale to the landscape
mapping.b_element: finite-dimensional ODE model that approximates the continuous dynamics by averaging over a finite number of habitat types

brief_justification: The only real overlap is the shared theme of biological populations in a spatially heterogeneous environment: paper A is a review of the physics of bacterial motility, growth, and sensing under two-way bacteria-soil feedback, with no specified governing dynamical system, whereas paper B's transferable machinery is a rigorous competition-persistence theory (integro-differential/ODE population dynamics with a mutual-invasibility criterion for coexistence). Paper A contains no second competing species, no invasion criterion, and no explicit governing equation, so the coexistence/invasibility machinery that drives B is absent, leaving only surface-level correspondences rather than a shared mechanism.
